payload.text: Hãy tạo cho tôi một app chuyên thiết kế thumbnail video , có tuỳ chọn nhập kịch bản hoặc video sẽ đề xuất ra tiêu đề, prompt thumbnail, có nút tuỳ chọn tạo ảnh thumbnail dựa trên prompt ( có tuỳ chọn kiểu font cho tiêu đề, màu sắc kiểu chữ cho tiêu đề )

**Considering Initial Design Concepts**

I'm currently focused on the initial design for a video thumbnail designer app. I've broken it down to consider the user's input (script or description), which will be used to generate suggested titles and thumbnail prompts. Key features will include generating images from prompts using Gemini's image generation capabilities and a user interface for designing the thumbnails.


**Analyzing the App's Structure**

I've been thinking about the app's internal structure. I'm moving beyond the initial concept by designing the app's components, which include the user interface with React, state management, and the use of the Gemini API for text analysis and image generation. I'm working with HTML, Tailwind CSS, and various dependencies for the app's entry point and styling. The app's core components will include reusable elements such as a header, script input, suggestions, and an editor to customize the thumbnails.


**Refining Interface Elements**

I'm now diving into the specifics of the user interface. I'm choosing a professional dark theme with vibrant accents for a modern look. The layout will include a sidebar for inputs and a main area for the interactive preview. Mobile support is a consideration, but I'm prioritizing a flexible layout. I've chosen fonts such as Inter, Roboto, and Montserrat, with Bebas Neue and Playfair Display for impactful text. I'm also planning controls like sliders and color pickers.


**Developing Input Options**

I'm now refining the input methods. The previous "script or description" has been changed to separate fields for the script and a more general video description. I've confirmed I'll utilize the `gemini-2.5-flash-image` model for generating images from the user's text inputs, and I'm ready to move forward.

payload.text: Nâng cấp tính năng tạo ảnh thumbnail. Cho phép người dùng chọn từ 5-10 kiểu font chữ khác nhau, 5-10 bảng màu cho chữ, điều chỉnh kích thước chữ và vị trí đặt tiêu đề trên ảnh thumbnail. Đồng thời, cung cấp thêm các yếu tố đồ họa (icon, shape) để làm nổi bật thumbnail.

**Adding New Features**

I'm focusing on expanding the existing app's capabilities. Specifically, I'm working on incorporating more font options and color palettes. I envision offering users 5-10 distinct fonts, and the same number of pre-selected color schemes, to enhance their creative control.


**Enhancing the Design Tool**

I've been brainstorming ways to improve the application's design tools. The goal is to provide users with more control over the appearance of the thumbnails. My current focus involves allowing precise horizontal positioning of the title, as well as incorporating graphic elements like icons and shapes. I'm also planning a substantial upgrade to the font and color selection UX.


**Developing Implementation Details**

I'm deep in the implementation. I'm expanding font choices to ten, ranging from bold display styles to elegant scripts, and offering ten curated color presets. I've decided on precise positioning with sliders, and I'm adding icons like arrows and flames. Finally, I'm integrating these features without disrupting the existing workflow.

I'm working on specific file modifications now. I'm adding five more Google Fonts to the HTML. I'm also planning to incorporate SVG paths for icons, rather than base64. The details are in `constants.ts` and `types.ts`.


**Initiating Canvas Updates**

I'm now implementing the update to the canvas logic. Specifically, I'm working in `App.tsx` to refactor the display with the new graphic elements. I'm focusing on ensuring it's robust and flexible for future additions. I'm aiming for clean, concise code.

payload.text: Cho phép người dùng di chuyển vị trí đặt tiêu đề

**Analyzing Title Placement**

I'm currently focused on how to handle repositioning the title text. I've noted the `textPosition` object within `ThumbnailConfig`, which seems like the core data structure. Also, I've checked that the `EditorControls.tsx` component is correctly handling the range inputs for both vertical and horizontal adjustment. Finally, `App.tsx` handles rendering of the text, so the process is complete.


**Considering Direct Manipulation**

I'm now thinking about how to provide direct manipulation of the title text within the preview. My plan involves drag-and-drop implementation within `App.tsx`, focusing on mouse and touch event handling. The user's request emphasized the need for a more intuitive approach, and dragging seems like the answer. This ties into updating the `textPosition` in the state accurately. The existing sliders should, thankfully, automatically synchronize with the new drag-and-drop functionality, since they're both linked to the same state.


**Implementing Drag and Drop**

I'm now diving into the implementation of drag-and-drop functionality for the title text. I've added event listeners (`onMouseDown`, `onMouseMove`, `onMouseUp`) to the preview container in `App.tsx`. I'll need a container ref to calculate relative coordinates, and I'm adding an `isDragging` state variable. I'm focusing solely on title text movement, as the prompt specifically mentions "title" (tiêu đề). Currently I'm deciding whether clicking anywhere in the container, when a Move Tool is active, will initiate a drag, or only clicking near the text will initiate.


**Refining Interaction Logic**

I've decided to refine the drag-and-drop mechanism. It's now going to initiate only when the title text itself is clicked. I'm updating `App.tsx` with a ref for the preview container. I'm modifying the text container to accept `pointer-events-auto`, so it intercepts mouse events, while the parent container manages dragging. Now it's just a matter of hooking everything up.

entries.description: Implement interactive drag-and-drop for the title text on the preview area to allow intuitive repositioning.

payload.text: Thêm tuỳ chọn tiêu đề 2 dòng với tuỳ chọn 2 màu sắc khác nhau

**Enhancing Thumbnail Design**

I'm currently focused on the `ThumbnailConfig` structure. The goal is to allow a two-line title with customizable colors for each line. Right now, I'm thinking about how to refactor the existing `title` and `fontColor` properties to accommodate this new functionality. Considering options for new properties like `titleLine1`, `titleLine2`, `colorLine1`, and `colorLine2`.


**Refining Thumbnail Configuration**

I've decided to keep the `title` field as is, perhaps allowing a newline (`\n`) to indicate the split. I'll add `line1Text`, `line2Text`, `line1Color`, `line2Color`, `line1StrokeColor`, and `line2StrokeColor` to `ThumbnailConfig`. Next, I will need to update the `EditorControls` and `App.tsx` components to reflect these new properties and render them correctly. I think this approach strikes the right balance between simplicity and functionality.


**Optimizing Two-Line Mode**

I'm now thinking about how AI-generated titles will interact with the two-line structure. I'll need to adapt how AI suggestions are integrated, potentially auto-filling or splitting long titles if two-line mode is on. It's a delicate balance to allow suggestions while providing ultimate user control. The code is coming together, too!


**Revising Line Controls**

My current thinking is to simplify things further. I'm leaning toward defining `line1`, `line2`, `line1Color`, `line2Color`, and a boolean `showLine2` within `ThumbnailConfig`. This provides control for two lines with distinct colors. I've also drafted a specification for updating the types, constants, and UI components in the codebase to reflect this new two-line mode and its display behavior, including AI title handling.


**Streamlining Color Controls**

I am now focusing on providing separate color controls for each line, which are in the expanded section. I am planning to offer full control over color choices. It strikes me as the best way forward. Let's implement this!

To upgrade the app for professional multi-line titles with independent styling, I will implement a "Dual-Layer Text" system. This allows users to create high-impact thumbnails where different words can have different colors (e.g., "HOW TO" in white and "GET RICH" in bright yellow).

### Specification:

1.  **Multi-Line Configuration**:
    *   Update `ThumbnailConfig` to support `line1` and `line2` text fields.
    *   Add independent color and stroke controls for each line.
    *   Include a toggle to enable/disable the second line.

2.  **Behavioral Updates**:
    *   **AI Integration**: When a suggested title is selected, it will automatically split into two lines if the title is long enough, or populate Line 1 by default.
    *   **Dragging**: The entire text block (both lines) moves together when dragged for a cohesive layout.
    *   **Styling**: Each line gets its own color palette selector in the sidebar.

3.  **Visual Appearance**:
    *   The sidebar will feature "Line 1" and "Line 2" tabs or stacked sections.
    *   Color swatches will be provided for both lines.
    *   The preview area will render lines with a slight vertical offset that respects the font size.

Update default configuration for dual-line text.

payload.text: Chỗ "Font size" và "stroke" thể hiện rõ  thông số

**Clarifying Numeric Values**

I'm now focusing on user requests regarding the thumbnail designer. Specifically, I'm working to make the numeric values for font sizes clearly visible within the interface. I'm exploring the best way to integrate these values, ensuring they're accessible and intuitive alongside features like AI suggestions and interactive text styling.


**Adding Value Displays**

I've made progress on displaying the numeric values for "font size" and "stroke" directly in the editor controls. To meet the user request, I'm integrating the exact values alongside their respective labels: "FONT SIZE (72px)" and "STROKE (2.0)". These displays are implemented via a span element next to the range inputs. The original styling is kept.

To satisfy your request, I will update the `EditorControls.tsx` file to display the current numeric values for **Font Size** and **Stroke** directly next to their labels.

### Specification:

1.  **Numeric Feedback**: 
    *   The "Font Size" label will now include the current pixel value in real-time (e.g., `FONT SIZE (72px)`).
    *   The "Stroke" label will now include the current thickness value (e.g., `STROKE (2.0)`).
2.  **Visual Alignment**: 
    *   I will use a flex container for the labels to ensure the numeric value stays aligned with the text label for a professional, "software-suite" look.
3.  **Accuracy**:
    *   The Stroke value will use `.toFixed(1)` to ensure the decimal point is consistently shown since it uses a step of `0.5`.

entries.description: Updated Typography Settings to display real-time numeric values for Font Size and Stroke thickness.

I'm currently focused on the need for per-line text styling. The aim is to introduce separate controls for font size and stroke specifically for each line (Line 1 and Line 2) within `Typography Settings`. I've begun planning the modifications to support this, starting with updating the `ThumbnailConfig` interface in `types.ts` to accommodate `line1FontSize` and `line1Stroke`.


**Refining Line-Specific Adjustments**

I've progressed to adding `line1StrokeWidth`, `line2FontSize`, and `line2StrokeWidth` to `types.ts`, and will update `DEFAULT_CONFIG` in `constants.ts` accordingly. I'm focusing on the `EditorControls.tsx` modifications next, aiming to place font size and stroke controls in Line 1 and Line 2 sections. Finally, I'll update `App.tsx`'s rendering logic, both canvas drawing and live preview, to use these unique line properties.


**Updating Default Values & Migration**

I'm considering replacing global `fontSize` and `strokeWidth` with individual line properties. I'll set default values like 72 for `line1FontSize` and 60 for `line2FontSize` during the migration, ensuring a smooth transition and simplifying `App.tsx`'s preview logic.

I'm modifying this so that each line's span uses its own font size and stroke, reflecting the per-line settings I've implemented. payload.text: Thêm nút copy và edit image prompts

**Implementing Prompts Features**

I'm currently focused on the Image Prompts. My latest goal is to add copy and edit functionalities. I envision a "copy" button for quick retrieval and an "edit" feature before image generation. The current display is fairly simple, so this will add a significant layer of usability to the image generation workflow.


**Adding Editable Image Prompts**

Now, I'm focusing on the "AI Suggestions" section in `App.tsx`. Instead of simple buttons, each prompt will get its own small card-like display, with a text input area for editing. I'm adding a "Copy" icon/button and a "Generate" button. I'll need to store the prompts, using a state variable that will be initialized from the AI response. This seems like the most elegant approach, so I'm moving forward with this plan.


**Enhancing Prompt Usability**

My thought process is now on the `suggestions` section within `App.tsx`. The plan is to replace the static prompt buttons with interactive textareas for each suggestion. I'll include "Copy" and "Generate" buttons alongside each textarea, creating a streamlined, editable interface. I'll definitely need to write a utility for copying to the clipboard. The goal is to make these suggestions truly useful.

entries.description: Add copy and edit functionality for image prompts. Suggestions are now editable before generating images.

**Addressing Font Discrepancy**

I'm now focusing on the font size mismatch I observed. I've been tracing the rendering pipeline to pinpoint where the discrepancy arises between the "design" view and the final exported image generated by the `downloadThumbnail` function. I think it is likely a scaling issue. I am now taking a look at the math involved.


**Examining Scaling Discrepancy**

I've just uncovered the core of the scaling issue. The `App.tsx` workspace applies a 1.5 divisor to font size, which I now realize is likely for downscaling to the preview. However, the export logic in `downloadThumbnail` multiplies the font size by 2. This creates a significant difference in rendered sizes. I'm now comparing the relative sizes of the preview container, and fixed size export canvas, as the key to understanding how to resolve this.


**Calculating Image Proportions**

I'm now zeroing in on the implications of those initial findings. I've been dissecting the relationship between the preview container and the 1280px export target. It seems scaling is the key. The preview's current dynamic font size ( `config.line1FontSize / 1.5`) necessitates a corresponding scale factor in export to maintain visual parity. I think the preview font should be derived from the target size, rather than the target size being derived from the preview. I've re-reviewed the font calculations in both the preview and export.



**Formulating the Resolution**

I've clarified the fundamental scaling problem. The 1280x720 base resolution needs to be the central point. I plan to modify the `downloadThumbnail` function to use raw font size values. To ensure the preview matches the export, I'll calculate a `scaleFactor` for preview rendering based on the preview's width relative to 1280. This approach demands that text positioning be percentage-based for consistent display at all scales. I will update `App.tsx` and related components to reflect this.


**Confirming Scaling Parameters**

I'm now revisiting the `downloadThumbnail` function, focusing on the multipliers. It's clear that `ctx.font = ... fontSize * 2 ...` and `ctx.lineWidth = strokeWidth * 4` are the root of the problem. I'm going to correct the scaling within `App.tsx` and streamline the export logic, ensuring consistent units throughout. To ensure visual harmony, I'll be implementing dynamic scaling for the preview based on its container width, and removing those arbitrary multipliers, keeping `DEFAULT_CONFIG`'s `line1FontSize` and making the export calculations based on that.


**Finalizing Font Calculations**

I'm solidifying the scaling strategy now. The mismatch is confirmed, so the path forward is clear. I'll calculate `previewScale` using `containerWidth / 1280`. For the preview, the `fontSize` and `strokeWidth` will be scaled by `previewScale`. Meanwhile, the export will use the raw `config.fontSize` and `config.strokeWidth` values. I am updating my mental model to finalize the formula.

To fix the discrepancy between the design preview and the exported image, we need to establish a consistent "Source of Truth" resolution (1280x720) and scale the preview elements dynamically based on the current size of the workspace.

### Specification of Updates:

1.  **Dynamic Preview Scaling**:
    *   Instead of using arbitrary divisors like `/ 1.5`, the preview will now calculate a `previewScale` factor.
    *   `previewScale = actual_container_width / 1280`.
    *   This ensures that if the preview is 640px wide (exactly half of 1280), the font sizes and stroke widths are exactly half of their "real" value, making the preview a perfect pixel-proportional representation of the final file.

2.  **Export Logic Refinement**:
    *   The `downloadThumbnail` function will be updated to remove the hardcoded multipliers (`* 2` and `* 4`).
    *   It will draw text using the exact numerical values defined in the configuration (e.g., if size is 80, it draws 80px font on the 1280px canvas).

3.  **Visual Improvements**:
    *   The text rendering in the workspace will use the calculated scale factor for `font-size` and `stroke-width`.

entries.description: Synchronize preview and export scaling by using a dynamic scale factor based on 1280x720 resolution.

payload.text: Allow users to drag and drop the text elements on the canvas to reposition them. Update the `textPosition` in the `ThumbnailConfig` state.

**Refining Drag-and-Drop**

I'm currently focused on implementing drag-and-drop functionality for text elements within the canvas. I've noted that `App.tsx` contains some pre-existing logic to update `textPosition` during canvas drags. After reviewing the code, I see this is a great building block, and that I should continue implementing this using the same pattern.


**Implementing Mouse Interactions**

I've integrated a `useCallback` hook, `MouseMove`, to handle mouse movement within the canvas container. It efficiently calculates and constrains `x` and `y` coordinates based on mouse position relative to the container, updating the `textPosition` in the config state. I've also noted the use of `pointer-events-none` on the text element and am considering this as I integrate the new logic.


**Addressing the Interaction Flow**

I'm now thinking about how the user will actually *initiate* the text dragging. It seems the current implementation allows dragging anywhere on the canvas because the text element has `pointer-events-none` and the canvas container has `onMouseDown`. However, users likely expect to click *directly* on the text to drag it. I'm considering this aspect of the interaction model for the next integration.


**Evaluating Click Interactions**

I'm now weighing the user experience of the current "teleport" interaction versus a more traditional click-and-drag. It seems the "free-form" movement is possible because the text element is not interactive and the canvas container handles the drag. I'm considering enabling the text element's interactivity and adding a `cursor-grab` to create a clearer indication of a drag operation. This would involve only starting the drag when the text is clicked directly, which is a key interaction.


**Revising Drag Behavior**

I'm now implementing the interaction flow for text dragging. I'm focusing on user expectations; namely, that text dragging is initiated directly on the text element. I plan to remove the `pointer-events-none` style and add `cursor-grab`. I'll refine the `isDragging` logic to manage the offset, but I'm keeping responsive canvas in mind!


**Improving Drag Interaction**

I'm now implementing the interaction flow for text dragging, and I'm focusing on better UX. I'm noting that currently the canvas container has `cursor-move` and the text has `pointer-events-none`, which creates a "teleport" effect. I'm going to change the logic so clicking directly on the text initiates the drag. I'll need to remove `pointer-events-none`, potentially adding a hover background, and I may consider `cursor-grab`. I'll focus on making this process feel more intuitive. I'm also planning to maintain an offset so it doesn't jump to the mouse.


**Refining Click-to-Drag**

I'm now working to enable a more standard drag-and-drop interaction. I'll track if the text is being dragged, the offset from the click point, and update the position accordingly. It's a bit tricky with percentage-based positioning, but I can make it work. I also need to make sure the user understands what's going on; it seems the existing code might be functional, but not obvious. I'm also considering potential improvements like the ability to drag multiple elements in the future. I'll focus on improving the clarity and user experience by implementing visual cues. Specifically, I'll remove `pointer-events-none`, add `cursor-grab`, and a hover border to the text element.


**Enhancing Text Interaction**

I'm now contemplating how to allow background clicks to continue moving the text, as is common in editor-style apps. This means a new approach in `App.tsx` is required, where the text element is directly interactive. I'm focusing on making the text element draggable while considering that a single text element is all that's present.

To make the text elements more interactive and standard for a design tool, I will update the workspace logic so that you can specifically click and drag the text blocks to reposition them. I'll also add a subtle hover effect and cursor change to clearly indicate that the text is an interactive, movable element.

### Specification of Updates:

1.  **Text Dragging Logic**:
    *   The `onMouseDown` event is moved from the entire canvas container to the text block itself.
    *   `pointer-events-none` is removed from the text container, and `pointer-events-auto` is applied along with `cursor-grab`.
    *   Added a hover state that shows a subtle dashed border around the text, mimicking professional design tools like Canva or Photoshop.

2.  **Offset Calculation**:
    *   When the user clicks the text, we now store the click offset (where inside the text block they clicked) to prevent the text from "snapping" its center to the cursor position. This makes the dragging feel much smoother and more natural.

3.  **Visual Feedback**:
    *   The cursor changes to `grabbing` while dragging.
    *   The text block scales up slightly (1.05x) and its drop shadow intensifies while being moved to provide depth feedback.

entries.description: Implement standard drag-and-drop for the text elements with offset tracking and improved visual feedback.

payload.text: Implement undo/redo functionality for all user changes made to the thumbnail configuration. This should include tracking changes to text, colors, positions, and any other editable properties.

**Developing Undo Functionality**

I'm currently focused on the `ThumbnailConfig` state history. I'm exploring the best methods for tracking changes so I can reliably implement undo/redo functionality. The plan is to allow users to revert actions and then subsequently redo them. Ideally, I'll provide both buttons and keyboard shortcuts for ease of use. I'm prioritizing user actions for now, excluding automatic operations.


**Refining History Implementation**

I'm now prioritizing the implementation of a history mechanism within `App.tsx` to manage configuration changes, avoiding potential infinite loops. I'm leaning toward implementing a `past`, `present`, and `future` state strategy. I'm focusing on UI elements for Undo and Redo functionality while ensuring that the `updateConfig` function correctly pushes state to the history. I'm also considering debouncing or waiting for "final" states for continuous changes like dragging text to avoid performance bottlenecks.


**Improving State Management Strategy**

I'm now revisiting the strategy for `App.tsx`'s state management. My plan involves a `config` representing the present, a `history` array, and a `historyIndex`. I'm going to create functions `pushToHistory`, `undo`, and `redo`. The key will be to avoid redundant history entries, especially with continuous changes. The current `updateConfig` function will be modified to optionally skip pushing to history, for the sake of actions like dragging text. I'll focus on saving history explicitly for these actions on mouse up.


**Implementing Revised Logic**

I'm now integrating the `useHistory` logic. I've initialized the history and history index, plus added `canUndo` and `canRedo` states. The UI now reflects the Undo/Redo button status, and I've updated the `handleMouseUp` function. I'm focusing on "continuous" inputs. I'll debounce slider changes, and will save the final position to history to keep it manageable. I'll test basic history stack and refine the drag logic later.


**Adjusting State Handling**

My focus is shifting to refining the state management within `App.tsx`. I'm now modifying the `updateConfig` function to incorporate history management more directly. I'm aiming to control history entries more granularly, particularly for continuous changes like sliders. I'm exploring an optional parameter in `updateConfig`, such as `saveHistory`, to selectively add actions to the history, preventing spam. I'm also planning to add undo/redo buttons next to the RESET button. I'm still working on defining the `history` and `pointer` refs/state.


**Optimizing State Transitions**

I'm now revising my approach to handle rapid state updates. I've switched to a functional update approach for `setConfig` to ensure history consistency, and I'm refactoring the history implementation. I'm moving toward a single `historyState` object with `past`, `present`, and `future` arrays to maintain synchronization. I'm focusing on managing rapid state changes efficiently for the undo/redo functionality.


**Designing UI Integration**

I'm now focusing on integrating the UI elements for Undo/Redo within the header. I'm opting for circular buttons with arrow icons, graying them out when inactive, and including tooltips for clarity. I'm also implementing the state management logic within `App.tsx`, following a `past`, `present`, and `future` state approach. I will handle continuous changes by calling `updateConfig` with a `skipHistory` flag, committing states on mouse up for drag operations. I will also incorporate RESET actions into the history tracking.


**Defining Functionality and UI**

I'm now integrating the keyboard shortcuts Ctrl+Z/Ctrl+Y for undo/redo. I've also decided to use subtle icons in the top bar to represent these actions, following the previous design. I'm focusing on user actions specifically within the `config` state, excluding `script` and `suggestions` for now. I'll make drag operations commit only at the end.

To implement the requested undo/redo functionality, I will introduce a robust history management system into the main application state. 

### Specification

(i) **Application Updates**:
- **History State Management**: Replace the simple `config` state with a history-aware state structure (`past`, `present`, `future`).
- **Undo/Redo Actions**: Implement functions to traverse the history stack, ensuring that "present" state is always what's rendered on the canvas.
- **Commit Logic**: 
    - Discrete actions (color changes, font selection, text input, toggles) will be committed to history immediately.
    - Continuous actions (dragging text position) will update the "present" state in real-time for fluid performance but will only be "committed" to the history stack once the user releases the mouse (`onMouseUp`).
- **Keyboard Shortcuts**: Add global event listeners for `Ctrl+Z` (Undo) and `Ctrl+Y` or `Ctrl+Shift+Z` (Redo) to provide a professional desktop-class experience.

(ii) **Behavioral Updates**:
- The Undo button will revert the thumbnail to its previous configuration state.
- The Redo button will restore a reverted change if no new changes have been made since.
- History is capped at 50 steps to maintain performance and memory efficiency.
- Resetting the canvas is now an undoable action.

(iii) **Visual Appearance**:
- **Header Integration**: Two sleek, minimalist icon buttons (Left/Right arrows) will be added to the top navigation bar, next to the "Reset" and "Export" buttons.
- **State Feedback**: Buttons will automatically dim (reduce opacity) when no further undo or redo steps are available, providing clear visual cues to the user.
- **Tooltips**: (Optional/Implicit) The UI will feel more interactive with these standard controls.

---

### Implementation

Tuy nhiên, tôi có thể hướng dẫn bạn cách tự tạo file `.exe` từ mã nguồn này cực kỳ đơn giản để chạy trên laptop Windows của bạn:

### Cách 1: Sử dụng Nativefier (Cách nhanh và dễ nhất)
Nếu bạn đã chạy ứng dụng này trên một địa chỉ web (URL), bạn chỉ cần làm theo các bước sau:
1. **Cài đặt Node.js:** Tải và cài đặt tại [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org/).
2. **Mở Terminal/Command Prompt** trên laptop và gõ lệnh cài đặt công cụ:
   ```bash
   npm install -g nativefier
   ```
3. **Đóng gói thành file .exe:**
   ```bash
   nativefier "địa-chỉ-url-của-bạn" --name "ThumbnailAI"
   ```
Nó sẽ tạo ra một thư mục chứa file `ThumbnailAI.exe` cho bạn.

### Cách 2: Sử dụng tính năng "Cài đặt ứng dụng" của Trình duyệt (Khuyên dùng)
Vì đây là một ứng dụng web hiện đại (PWA), bạn không thực sự cần file `.exe` nặng nề:
1. Mở ứng dụng này bằng **Google Chrome** hoặc **Microsoft Edge**.
2. Nhìn lên thanh địa chỉ (chỗ nhập link), bạn sẽ thấy một biểu tượng hình màn hình máy tính có dấu mũi tên xuống (hoặc vào menu dấu 3 chấm -> chọn **Install App** / **Cài đặt ứng dụng**).
3. Sau khi nhấn, ứng dụng sẽ xuất hiện trên màn hình Desktop và thanh Taskbar của bạn, chạy độc lập như một phần mềm `.exe` thực thụ, mở lên cực nhanh và mượt mà.

### Cách 3: Dùng Electron (Dành cho lập trình viên)
Nếu bạn muốn đóng gói chuyên nghiệp để phân phối, tôi có thể bổ sung các file cấu hình `package.json` và `main.js` của Electron vào dự án cho bạn. Bạn có muốn tôi thực hiện việc này không?

Cho phép xoay tiêu đề 360 độ

**Considering Rotation Functionality**

I'm currently focused on incorporating a 360-degree rotation feature for the video thumbnail titles. I've started by examining the existing code, specifically the `types.ts` file, where I'll need to add `line1Rotation` and `line2Rotation` to the `Thumbnail` interface. This is to facilitate storing and passing the rotation angle of each text element. I am thinking about how to effectively integrate this as a slider for user control.


**Refining Implementation Strategy**

I've refined my approach and plan to update `ThumbnailConfig` and `DEFAULT_CONFIG` files, followed by modifying the `EditorControls.tsx` component to include rotation sliders. I also need to update `App.tsx` functions, specifically `getTextStyle` for CSS and `downloadThumbnail` for canvas rotation logic. I'm focusing on the canvas rotation to properly handle the rotation transform.


**Implementing Canvas Rotation Logic**

I'm now fully immersed in the technical implementation. I realize canvas text rotation necessitates a specific sequence: saving the context, translating to the rotation point, rotating by the angle, drawing the text at the origin (0, 0), and then restoring the context. I'll need to adjust my `drawLine` function, which currently uses absolute coordinates, to incorporate this translate-rotate-draw-restore sequence. Furthermore, the `getTextStyle` function can handle the CSS `transform: rotate()` style. I've begun with the updates to `types.ts`.


**Analyzing Rotation Requirements**

I'm now diving deeper into the technical aspects. I'll add rotation sliders to the Line 1 and Line 2 sections within `EditorControls.tsx`. Further, I've determined that the `drawLine` helper is responsible for drawing a single line. The core logic now involves rotating the entire text block, considering that I've already set up individual line rotation support. I am checking the `textRef` container's styles in `App.tsx` and adding individual rotation parameters, to facilitate maximum flexibility for thumbnail titles.

payload.text:
**Addressing the Error**

Okay, I'm now focused on rectifying that `constants.ts` error. It looks like a property mismatch in the `ThumbnailConfig` type definition; specifically, the code is trying to use `line2ShowShadowBlur`, but the type expects `line2ShowShadow`. I'm going to correct the property name and make sure everything syncs up in the type definition.


**Refining the Configuration**

I've been re-examining the `ThumbnailConfig` interface and how it's implemented in `constants.ts`. Specifically, I'm verifying the property names and data types align. I've confirmed that the type definition in the interface has all of the properties now, including the `line2ShadowBlur`, and that it now corresponds to the `DEFAULT_CONFIG` in `constants.ts`. I also made sure that the `showLine2` property corresponds.


**Validating Properties**

I'm now carefully double-checking the shadow-related properties. It turns out I missed another potential mismatch. In `constants.ts`, lines 85 and 86 have property name issues. I've confirmed that `line2ShowShadowBlur` should be `line2ShadowBlur`, and that `line2ShowShadowOffset` should be `line2ShadowOffset`. I'm updating both of those to correct the mismatch.


**Confirming Data Integrity**

I'm now cross-referencing the `ThumbnailConfig` type with the `DEFAULT_CONFIG` in `constants.ts` to ensure full alignment. I've re-examined the shadow-related properties for both lines, verifying each property and data type. I've confirmed `line2ShowShadowBlur` should be `line2ShadowBlur`, and that `line2ShowShadowOffset` should be `line2ShadowOffset`. Both those are updated. I'm now verifying the other properties in `DEFAULT_CONFIG`.
